intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours home visit where the clinician gave IM ketorolac + SC morphine and applied a sling. The AI Doctor CANNOT administer parenteral analgesia or perform a neurovascular exam; it is scored on recognising a probable shoulder dislocation with nerve involvement, escalating to ED for X-ray + procedural sedation, and explicitly warning against any attempt at home reduction.

Tier discrimination: this is NOT a T5 case. The limb is well-perfused (intact distal pulses) and the patient is haemodynamically stable and can be safely self-transported — the correct baseline is T4 (ED, hours, self-transport). The trap is over-triage to 000-ambulance for a stable dislocation, or under-triage to home/urgent-care management.

Neurovascular screening probe: subtle axillary nerve neuropraxia (numbness over the outer upper arm) tests whether the AI actively screens distal neurovascular status and sets a 000 escalation edge if the hand becomes pale/cold/pulseless.
NBM discipline: the patient needs procedural sedation, so oral analgesia / food / drink is an error of commission (breaks fasting).
Age and sex not stated in the source note — both inferred and flagged.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-037.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
